headline: New approach targets Alzheimer's by repairing the brain's waste system
meta_description: Researchers discover a copper compound that repairs the brain's natural clearing system, offering a fresh direction in Alzheimer's treatment.
summary_short: Instead of attacking toxic proteins directly, a new copper compound helps the brain's own waste-clearing system work better.
body:
Most Alzheimer's research has focused on eliminating toxic proteins in the brain. But scientists at Monash University's Institute of Pharmaceutical Sciences are exploring a different path: what if the problem isn't just the proteins themselves, but the brain's ability to clear them away?

Their work, published in ACS Chemical Neuroscience, found that a copper compound called Cu(ATSM) can repair the brain's natural waste-clearing mechanisms. By supporting the brain's own defences rather than fighting the disease head-on, this approach could open new possibilities for treating Alzheimer's and offers hope for a more effective intervention strategy.
